skill.name: styling-with-shadcn
skill.description: Build accessible shadcn/ui interfaces in Next.js with practical examples for forms, dialogs, tables, sidebars, dark mode, and theme customization.
skill.summary: Create polished shadcn/ui components and Next.js interface patterns.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found the weak-crypto and Ruby backtick alerts are false positives from markdown code fences, color tokens, and UI examples. The skill does contain legitimate package installation commands and one Google Fonts import, so publication is reasonable with a supply-chain and external-network warning. No prompt-injection text, credential exfiltration, destructive command, or malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Remote Package Installation Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run npx shadcn@latest, npm install next-themes, font package installation, and the local verify script. These commands are expected for a shadcn/ui setup guide, but they execute package-manager workflows and should be reviewed before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly present and would invoke local tooling or package managers. Context shows they are normal setup instructions, not malicious execution.

low_findings.title: External Font Import in CSS Example
low_findings.description: The theme reference includes a Google Fonts @import URL. This is a real external network dependency if copied into an application, but it does not send secrets or perform suspicious requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded HTTPS URL is visible in a CSS import. It is a common font dependency and no evidence of data exfiltration was found.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives in Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby backtick execution, weak cryptographic algorithm, and system reconnaissance alerts are not supported by semantic review. They come from markdown code fences, inline code formatting, UI words such as destructive or blocked, CSS colors, and ordinary component examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found documentation and UI sample code, not shell backtick execution or cryptographic functions. No prompt-injection or malicious behavior was present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Manager Commands in Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: Commands that install or execute package-manager tooling are documented for setup. Review package names and versions before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and relevant to the skill purpose. The risk is supply-chain exposure, not evidence of malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Stylesheet Dependency
dangerous_patterns.description: The CSS example references Google Fonts, creating an external browser request if adopted by a project.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URL is directly present and its purpose is a font import. No hidden payload or credential transfer was found.

content.value_statement: Teams need consistent, accessible React interfaces without rebuilding common UI patterns. This skill provides shadcn/ui setup guidance, component recipes, validation patterns, and theme examples for Next.js projects.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to initialize shadcn/ui and add common components in a Next.js project.
Provides examples for buttons, forms, dialogs, alert dialogs, cards, sidebars, tables, and toast notifications.
Demonstrates react-hook-form with Zod validation for accessible form workflows.
Includes dark mode setup using next-themes and shadcn/ui theme conventions.
Documents a custom TaskFlow visual theme with Tailwind variables, typography, badges, and layout patterns.
limitations: The guidance is focused on React and Next.js projects, not other frontend frameworks.
Examples may need updates when shadcn/ui, Tailwind, or Next.js APIs change.
The skill does not generate a complete application architecture by itself.
Package installation commands should be reviewed before running in production repositories.

use_cases.title: Build a Product Form
use_cases.description: Create a validated task, profile, or settings form with shadcn/ui fields and Zod rules.

use_cases.title: Standardize Dashboard Components
use_cases.description: Apply consistent cards, tables, sidebars, dialogs, badges, and toast patterns across a Next.js dashboard.

use_cases.title: Create a Themed Interface System
use_cases.description: Adapt Tailwind variables, typography, status colors, and dark mode behavior for a branded application.

prompt_templates.title: Add Basic shadcn/ui Components
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new Next.js interface.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add shadcn/ui buttons, cards, and form inputs to my Next.js page. Keep the result accessible and simple.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Validated Form
prompt_templates.scenario: Build a production form with validation and clear errors.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a shadcn/ui form with react-hook-form and Zod validation for these fields: [list fields]. Include loading and error states.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Dashboard Layout
prompt_templates.scenario: Combine navigation, tables, badges, and actions.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to build a dashboard layout with a sidebar, data table, action menu, status badges, and confirmation dialog for destructive actions.

prompt_templates.title: Apply a Custom Theme System
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced theming for a branded application.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to adapt the TaskFlow theme patterns into my Next.js app. Define dark mode variables, typography, badge variants, and responsive dashboard sections.

output_examples.input: A request to create a task form with title, description, priority, and submit behavior.
output_examples.output: A concise implementation plan for a shadcn/ui form, including validation rules, field layout, disabled submit state, and error handling guidance.

output_examples.input: A request to add a dashboard sidebar and sortable task table.
output_examples.output: A component breakdown covering sidebar navigation, table columns, pagination controls, status badges, and edit or delete actions.

output_examples.input: A request to add dark mode and a custom visual theme.
output_examples.output: A theme checklist with Tailwind variables, font choices, status colors, button variants, and reduced-motion considerations.

best_practices: Install only the shadcn/ui components needed for the current workflow.
Keep validation schemas close to forms and reuse shared types where possible.
Review generated component code for accessibility, keyboard behavior, and responsive layout.
anti_patterns: Do not paste every example into an application without removing unused components.
Do not run package installation commands without reviewing package names and versions.
Do not override shadcn/ui defaults in ways that weaken focus states or semantic structure.

faq.question: What projects does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports React and Next.js projects that use shadcn/ui, Tailwind CSS, Radix UI, and related component patterns.

faq.question: Can it help with form validation?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes patterns for react-hook-form, Zod schemas, form fields, validation messages, and submit states.

faq.question: Does it include complete component examples?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for forms, data tables, sidebars, dialogs, cards, dark mode, and a custom theme.

faq.question: Does it work outside Next.js?
faq.answer: It may provide useful React ideas, but the instructions are written for Next.js and shadcn/ui conventions.

faq.question: Does it install packages automatically?
faq.answer: No. It documents package commands that a developer or agent may choose to run after review.

faq.question: What should I review before using it?
faq.answer: Review package commands, copied component code, external font imports, and theme changes before applying them to a repository.
